claims.claim: Bot API 10.0 is the current Telegram Bot API version (released May 8, 2026), with 9.6 and 9.5 as the prior two minor versions.
In a single chat, a bot should not send more than ~1 message per second.
claims.quote: In a single chat, avoid sending more than one message per second.

claims.claim: In a group, a bot cannot send more than 20 messages per minute.
claims.quote: In a group, bots are not be able to send more than 20 messages per minute.

claims.claim: For bulk notifications a bot cannot broadcast more than ~30 messages per second unless paid broadcasts are enabled. 429 errors result from exceeding the limit.
claims.quote: For bulk notifications, bots are not able to broadcast more than about 30 messages per second, unless they enable paid broadcasts.

claims.claim: Paid broadcasts allow up to 1,000 messages per second at 0.1 Stars per message above the free 30/sec, requiring a 100,000 Stars minimum balance and 100,000 monthly active users.
claims.quote: Enabling paid broadcasts allows bots to send up to 1000 messages per second... costs 0.1 Stars per message... minimum bot balance of 100,000 Stars and 100,000 monthly active users.

claims.claim: ILS is a supported currency in the Bot Payments API with a minimum of ₪3.68 and maximum of ₪36,788.20 per invoice.
claims.quote: Code: ILS, Title: Israeli New Sheqel, Min amount: ₪ 3.68, Max amount: ₪ 36,788.20

claims.claim: Israeli payment processors (Tranzila, Meshulam, Cardcom) are not on Telegram's officially-supported provider list connectable through @BotFather as of mid-2026; Stripe is the primary documented integration.
claims.notes: WebFetch of the official docs confirms no Israeli solek is listed by name as a Telegram-integrated provider; only Stripe (TEST/LIVE modes) is identified explicitly.

claims.claim: Israel's Anti-Spam Law is Section 30a of the Communications (Telecommunications and Broadcasting) Law, 5742-1982, added by Amendment 40 (2008). Statutory damages up to ₪1,000 per message without need to prove damage.
claims.notes: Kol-Zchut summary of Section 30a(i): פיצויים שאינם תלויים בנזק, בסכום שלא יעלה על 1,000 ₪ בגין כל הודעה.

claims.claim: Knowingly sending unsolicited commercial messages is a criminal offense; the corporate fine ceiling as of 2026 is approximately ₪226,000 (updated annually).
claims.notes: Search result attributed to Israeli legal commentary; figure is updated periodically. Hedged in skill text as approximate.

claims.claim: Section 30a violations are listed in the Second Schedule of the Class Actions Law, enabling class actions over mass mailings.
claims.notes: Hebrew Wikipedia: 'התיקון הוסיף את פרט 12 לתוספת השנייה לחוק תובענות ייצוגיות, תשס"ו-2006'.

claims.claim: Bit has a developer portal at developer.bitpay.co.il for merchant integrations.
claims.notes: Confirmed via web search; portal exists with sandbox and documentation.

claims.claim: Bit caps payments at ₪5,000 per transaction and ₪20,000 per month per merchant, supports ILS only, single-payment only (no installments, no subscriptions).
claims.notes: Confirmed via independent search results citing Bit's merchant terms. Caps are subject to change; skill flags this as the 2026 state.

claims.claim: PayBox is owned by Israel Discount Bank, has about 2.5M users, and has no public merchant API as of 2026.
claims.notes: Wikipedia confirms Discount Bank ownership; absence of public merchant API confirmed by lack of any developer portal found in web search.

claims.claim: Tranzila is an Israeli payment processor operating since 1999 with three integration modes (Iframe, Hosted Fields, API v2).
claims.notes: Tranzila docs confirm the three integration modes and PCI scope (SAQ-A / SAQ-A-EP / SAQ-D).

claims.claim: Tranzila has community-built Telegram integrations via Make.com (formerly Integromat) automation workflows.
claims.notes: Make.com integrations page confirms a Tranzila + Telegram template exists.

claims.claim: Telegram Mini Apps are a mainstream commerce surface in 2026; Telegram crossed 1 billion monthly active users, with TON-based in-chat wallets in widespread use.
claims.notes: Official Telegram Mini Apps docs are the canonical reference; user-count claim sourced from Telegram public communications and industry coverage (e.g. omisoft.net 2026 Mini Apps guide).

claims.claim: Mini Apps are configured per-bot through @BotFather (/newapp or /myapps -> Edit Bot -> Configure Mini App), served from the developer's own HTTPS URL.
Posting to a Telegram channel is a single API call regardless of subscriber count and is not subject to the bot send-rate broadcast limit; per-user DM sendMessage calls are.
claims.notes: FAQ rate limits explicitly govern bot 'sendMessage' calls; a single channel post is one such call. Channels distribute the post to subscribers via Telegram's own infrastructure.
